title: Dog Vomiting Fresh Blood: Emergency Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is vomiting fresh blood, it is a high-urgency emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is necessary and how to help your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Seeing fresh blood in your dog’s vomit is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention. Safely taking a photo of the vomit can help the veterinarian quickly assess the severity and speed up life-saving treatment.

What is hematemesis and what does fresh blood in my dog's vomit mean?

Finding fresh, bright red blood in your dog's vomit is a condition medically known as hematemesis. Unlike digested blood, which may look like dark coffee grounds, fresh blood indicates active bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract, including the mouth, esophagus, or stomach lining.

Is seeing fresh blood in my dog's vomit a veterinary emergency?

  • Fresh blood in vomit is a high-urgency symptom that requires immediate veterinary intervention.
  • Internal bleeding can lead to rapid blood loss, shock, and organ failure.
  • Causes can range from the ingestion of toxins (like rat poison) and foreign objects to severe gastric ulcers or clotting disorders.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to worsen; contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How can taking a photo of the vomit help the veterinarian?

  • If you can safely do so, take a clear photo of the vomit before cleaning it up.
  • Showing this photo to the triage staff or veterinarian provides vital information regarding the volume, color, and consistency of the blood.
  • This visual evidence helps the medical team assess the severity of the situation more accurately and can significantly speed up the diagnostic process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, vomiting may contain frank blood (hematemesis) or have the appearance of coffee grounds, indicating a bleeding lesion such as a gastric ulcer or neoplasm. Hematemesis warrants immediate investigation, especially if accompanied by melena (black, tarry feces), systemic illness, or abnormalities on abdominal palpation. Diagnostic efforts should include a thorough history, clinical findings, and initial symptomatic treatment, followed by a CBC, serum biochemical profile, urinalysis, and potentially more specific clinicopathologic testing and diagnostic imaging. The presence of hematemesis or melena necessitates a specific diagnosis if clinical signs do not resolve within two days of symptomatic therapy, and further investigation into potential foreign objects or toxins.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 156)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog water or food after they vomit blood?
faqs.answer: No. You should withhold all food and water until your dog has been examined by a veterinarian, as ingestion could worsen the irritation or interfere with potential emergency surgery.

faqs.question: Does a small amount of blood still count as an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even a small amount of fresh blood can be the early warning sign of a major internal issue. It is always best to err on the side of caution with gastrointestinal bleeding.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms I should watch for?
faqs.answer: Look for pale gums, lethargy, weakness, a distended abdomen, or black, tarry stools, as these are signs of severe blood loss and shock.
